articles.title: Keeping Birds Safe at the Beach
articles.summary: On the Isle of Man, a special area called Langness is being protected to help endangered birds. From March to August, people are asked to stay away from parts of the coast where birds like the ringed plover build their nests on the ground. Signs and ropes are put up to remind everyone to keep their distance. This is important because the nests are hard to see and can be easily disturbed by walkers or dogs. Manx BirdLife, a group that cares for birds, is leading this effort to make sure the birds can raise their babies safely. They want everyone to know that these places are special and need our help to stay safe for the birds.

articles.title: A New Fossil Ape Changes Where We Think Humans Came From
articles.summary: Scientists found a new fossil ape in Egypt that is about 17-18 million years old. This fossil, called Masripithecus, helps us learn more about where apes and humans started. Before, many scientists thought early apes came from East Africa, but this discovery suggests they might have come from northern Africa instead. The fossil shows that apes were diverse and spread to other places long ago. This finding is important because it tells us we need to look in new places to understand human origins better. It reminds us that science can change with new discoveries, and there is still much to learn about our past.

articles.title: Earthquake Video Shows Ground Splitting Fast
articles.summary: In March 2025, a big earthquake hit Myanmar, making the ground split open. Scientists got lucky because a security camera recorded it happening. They watched the video and saw the ground move sideways by 2.5 meters in just over a second. This is very fast! The earthquake was caused by a strike-slip fault, where two pieces of Earth's crust slide past each other. Usually, scientists use instruments far away to study earthquakes, but this video gave them a direct look. It helped them learn that the movement was quick and curved, not straight. This new way of watching earthquakes can help us understand them better and keep people safe in the future.

articles.title: Space News: Rockets, Moon Bases, and Secret Launches
articles.summary: This article talks about exciting space news. NASA is changing its plans for exploring the Moon and Mars. Instead of building a space station around the Moon called Gateway, NASA will focus on making a base on the Moon's surface. They also announced a new nuclear-powered rocket named SR-1 Freedom, which will help travel to Mars more efficiently. In other news, a German company called Isar Aerospace tried to launch its Spectrum rocket but had to stop because a boat got too close. Also, a secret missile was launched from Cape Canaveral in Florida, and people think it might be a test for a fast missile called hypersonic. Russia is also launching its own internet satellites like Starlink. These events show how busy space exploration is right now.

articles.title: Gut Bacteria Help Control Our Immune System
articles.summary: Scientists have found that tiny bacteria living in our gut can send special proteins into our body's cells. These proteins help control how our immune system works, which keeps us healthy. The research was done by a team from Helmholtz Munich and other universities. They discovered that even good bacteria, which are normally harmless, have tiny syringe-like parts that inject proteins. This is a new way bacteria talk to our cells. It might explain why some people get sick with diseases like Crohn's disease, where the gut gets inflamed. The study shows that bacteria are not just sitting in our gut; they are actively helping or sometimes causing problems by changing our immune responses. This could lead to new treatments in the future.

articles.title: Stars and Planets: How Space Weather Helps Find New Worlds
articles.summary: Scientists are learning about stars and planets in space. They study M dwarf stars, which are smaller and cooler than our Sun. Many of these stars have rocky planets like Earth, but those planets might not be good for life because they can be too hot or have too much radiation. A scientist named Luke Bouma found a new way to study space weather, which is like storms and winds from stars that affect planets. He looked at young stars that spin fast and get dimmer sometimes. He discovered that this dimming comes from big clouds of plasma, a type of hot gas, trapped by the star's magnetic field. These clouds form a doughnut shape called a torus. This torus acts like a natural space weather station, helping scientists understand how stars influence their planets. By studying this, they hope to find out if planets around M dwarf stars could support life. This discovery is exciting because it gives us a new tool to explore alien worlds.
